fact: September 15 sits exactly one week before the autumn equinox, when day and night become perfectly equal!
In the Northern Hemisphere, leaves are secretly starting their color-changing magic right now, even if you can't see it yet.
Monarch butterflies are typically in the middle of their epic 3,000-mile journey south, and some might be flying over your head today!
The sun rises about 2 minutes later than it did yesterday, and it's setting 2 minutes earlier too - darkness is sneaking up on us!
September 15 is smack dab in the middle of spider season - this is when house spiders come indoors looking for mates, not warmth like most people think.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, this date marks the heart of spring, when flowers are exploding into bloom everywhere.
Squirrels are in full panic mode right now, burying up to 25 nuts per day to prepare for winter!
September 15 falls during the peak migration time for ruby-throated hummingbirds, who can cross the entire Gulf of Mexico in one non-stop 18-hour flight.
In northern regions, the first frost can happen any day now, turning spider webs into glittering crystal art overnight.
This date marks exactly 258 days since New Year's Day and exactly 107 days until the end of the year.
September 15 is prime time for seeing the Northern Lights in places like Alaska and northern Canada, as the longer nights give more darkness for the aurora to shine.
